function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
The xAI merger became effective February 2, 2026 and the X merger March 28, 2025. For how many months of FY2025 was each entity included on an actual-close basis, and what analytical challenge does retrospective recast accounting create?
I'll analyze this question about the merger effective dates and retrospective recast accounting. Let me first verify the merger dates and accounting treatment from the SpaceX S-1 filing.
steps.reasoning_content:
The user is asking about the xAI merger (effective February 2, 2026) and the X merger (effective March 28, 2025), and wants to know:
1. For how many months of FY2025 was each entity included on an actual-close basis
2. What analytical challenge does retrospective recast accounting create

Let me think about this:

For FY2025 (calendar year 2025):
- xAI merger: effective February 2, 2026 - this is AFTER FY2025 ended, so xAI was NOT included on an actual-close basis for any months of FY2025
- X merger: effective March 28, 2025 - this means X was included from March 28, 2025 through December 31, 2025

Let me calculate the months for X:
- March 28, 2025 to December 31, 2025
- That's approximately 9 months (April through December = 9 full months, plus partial March)

Actually, let me be more precise. The merger closed March 28, 2025. So:
- March 28-31, 2025: ~4 days of March
- April through December: 9 full months
- Total: approximately 9 months and 4 days, or about 9.13 months

For xAI:
- Merger closed February 2, 2026
- FY2025 ended December 31, 2025
- xAI was NOT included on an actual-close basis for any part of FY2025 (0 months)

Now, the analytical challenge of retrospective recast accounting:
- Under common-control accounting, the consolidated financial statements are retrospectively recast to include both xAI and X Holdings for ALL historical periods presented
- This means even though xAI wasn't actually part of SpaceX until February 2, 2026, the FY2025 financials show xAI's results as if it had been consolidated for the entire year
- Similarly, X Holdings is shown for the entire FY2025 even though it only merged March 28, 2025
- This creates a challenge because the reported FY2025 figures don't reflect the actual economic reality of when these entities were part of SpaceX
- It makes it difficult to:
  - Compare year-over-year growth on a like-for-like basis
  - Understand the standalone SpaceX performance
  - Assess the actual contribution of each entity during the periods they were actually consolidated
  - The financials mix pre-merger and post-merger results as if they were always combined

Let me verify this by looking at the S-1 filing to confirm the merger dates and the recast accounting treatment.

Let me search the filing for relevant information.

results.content: SUCCESS: Loaded portable assets for key: spacex_s1

steps.reasoning_content: Let me retrieve information about the merger dates and the recast accounting treatment.

arguments.prompt: In {{spacex_s1}}, find the effective dates of the xAI merger and the X Holdings merger. Also find information about the retrospective recast accounting treatment under common-control accounting, including how the consolidated financial statements include xAI and X Holdings for historical periods.

results.content:
Based on the provided text, here are the effective dates and the details regarding the retrospective recast accounting treatment:

**Effective Dates of the Mergers:**
*   **xAI Merger:** Effective February 2, 2026, when SpaceX completed its acquisition of X.AI Holdings Corp. ("xAI").
*   **X Holdings Merger:** Effective March 28, 2025, when xAI completed its acquisition of X Holdings Corp. ("X") and X.AI Corp. 

**Retrospective Recast Accounting Treatment:**
*   **Common-Control Reorganizations:** The Mergers were accounted for as reorganizations of entities under common control because Elon Musk had a controlling financial interest in SpaceX, xAI, and X through his majority voting interest in each entity during the periods presented.
*   **Retrospective Combination:** The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast for all periods presented to include the historical results of xAI and X Holdings. The financial statements reflect the retrospective combination of the net assets of the entities at their historical carrying amounts.
*   **Goodwill and Intangibles:** No new goodwill or other intangible assets have been recorded as a result of the mergers.
*   **Intercompany Transactions:** All historical related party transactions between the entities have been eliminated in consolidation.
*   **Capital Stock and Shareholders' Equity:** The capital stock and shareholders' equity reflect a continuation of the historical SpaceX capital stock combined with the historical capital stock of X and xAI, adjusted by the respective exchange ratios used to effect the Mergers (except for xAI’s historical redeemable convertible preferred stock through the date of the xAI Merger). 
*   **Change in Reporting Entity:** This retrospective presentation constitutes a change in reporting entity. Because the consolidated financial statements already reflect this reorganization for all periods presented, separate financial statements of xAI and X are not provided.

arguments.prompt: In {{spacex_s1}}, what is SpaceX's fiscal year end? Is it December 31? Also confirm the periods presented in the consolidated financial statements (e.g., years ended December 31, 2025, 2024, 2023).

results.content:
Based on the provided text, yes, SpaceX's fiscal year ends on **December 31**. 

The periods presented in the consolidated financial statements are confirmed as follows:

**Audited Consolidated Financial Statements:**
* **Consolidated Balance Sheets:** As of December 31, 2025 and 2024
* **Consolidated Statements of Operations:** For the Years Ended December 31, 2025, 2024, and 2023
* **Consolidated Statements of Comprehensive Income (Loss):** For the Years Ended December 31, 2025, 2024, and 2023
* **Consolidated Statements of Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock and Shareholders' Equity:** For the Years Ended December 31, 2025, 2024, and 2023
* **Consolidated Statements of Cash Flows:** For the Years Ended December 31, 2025, 2024, and 2023

**Unaudited Interim Consolidated Financial Statements:**
* **Consolidated Balance Sheets:** As of March 31, 2026 and December 31, 2025
* **Consolidated Statements of Operations:** For the Three Months Ended March 31, 2026 and 2025
* **Consolidated Statements of Comprehensive Loss:** For the Three Months Ended March 31, 2026 and 2025
* **Consolidated Statements of Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock and Shareholders' Equity:** For the Three Months Ended March 31, 2026 and 2025
* **Consolidated Statements of Cash Flows:** For the Three Months Ended March 31, 2026 and 2025

steps.message: Now I have all the information needed. Let me calculate the precise number of months each entity was included on an actual-close basis during FY2025.
steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have all the information I need. Let me calculate the months:

FY2025 = January 1, 2025 to December 31, 2025 (12 months)

**xAI merger:**
- Effective date: February 2, 2026
- This is AFTER FY2025 ended (December 31, 2025)
- So xAI was included on an actual-close basis for 0 months of FY2025

**X Holdings merger:**
- Effective date: March 28, 2025
- From March 28, 2025 to December 31, 2025
- Let me calculate the months:
  - March 28-31, 2025: 4 days (out of 31 days in March) = ~0.13 months
  - April 2025: 1 month
  - May 2025: 1 month
  - June 2025: 1 month
  - July 2025: 1 month
  - August 2025: 1 month
  - September 2025: 1 month
  - October 2025: 1 month
  - November 2025: 1 month
  - December 2025: 1 month
  - Total: 9 full months + ~0.13 months = ~9.13 months

Let me calculate this more precisely:
- From March 28, 2025 to December 31, 2025
- March 28 to March 31 = 4 days (March has 31 days, so 4/31 = 0.129 months)
- April to December = 9 months
- Total = 9 + 0.129 = 9.129 months ≈ 9.13 months

Or we could say approximately 9 months (if we count full months only, April through December = 9 months, with a partial March).

Actually, let me think about this differently. The question asks "for how many months of FY2025 was each entity included on an actual-close basis."

For X Holdings:
- Closed March 28, 2025
- So from March 28, 2025 through December 31, 2025
- That's 9 months and 4 days
- Or approximately 9.13 months

For xAI:
- Closed February 2, 2026
- FY2025 ended December 31, 2025
- So xAI was NOT included on an actual-close basis for any part of FY2025
- 0 months

Now for the analytical challenge of retrospective recast accounting:

The key challenge is that the consolidated financial statements present FY2025 (and all historical periods) as if xAI and X had always been part of SpaceX, even though:
- xAI didn't actually merge until February 2, 2026 (after FY2025 ended)
- X didn't actually merge until March 28, 2025 (so only ~9 months of FY2025)

This creates several analytical challenges:
1. **No standalone SpaceX baseline**: The reported FY2025 figures don't reflect standalone SpaceX operations - they include xAI for the full year (even though xAI wasn't actually consolidated until 2026) and X for the full year (even though X was only consolidated from March 28, 2025).

2. **Year-over-year comparability is distorted**: Because all historical periods are recast to include both entities, you can't see the actual growth trajectory of standalone SpaceX, nor can you easily isolate the contribution of xAI or X to the consolidated results.

3. **Mixing of pre-merger and post-merger economics**: The financials combine periods when these were separate economic entities (with related-party transactions eliminated) as if they were always one entity. This obscures the actual timing of when economic benefits were realized.

4. **Inability to assess actual contribution during consolidation periods**: Since xAI is shown for all of FY2025 even though it wasn't actually consolidated until 2026, and X is shown for all of FY2025 even though it was only consolidated from March 28, 2025, an analyst cannot determine from the face of the financials what the actual consolidated results were during the periods these entities were genuinely part of SpaceX.

5. **No separate financial statements provided**: The filing explicitly states that "Because the consolidated financial statements already reflect this reorganization for all periods presented, separate financial statements of xAI and X are not provided." This means there's no way to isolate the individual entity contributions.

6. **Pro forma vs. actual distinction lost**: Under common-control accounting, the recast presentation looks like a "pro forma" combined presentation but is presented as if it were actual historical results, which can mislead readers into thinking these were always combined entities.

Let me also calculate the precise number of days/months for X Holdings:

From March 28, 2025 to December 31, 2025:
- March: 31 - 28 + 1 = 4 days (March 28, 29, 30, 31)
- April: 30 days
- May: 31 days
- June: 30 days
- July: 31 days
- August: 31 days
- September: 30 days
- October: 31 days
- November: 30 days
- December: 31 days
- Total days: 4 + 30 + 31 + 30 + 31 + 31 + 30 + 31 + 30 + 31 = 279 days

FY2025 total days: 365 days

So X was included for 279/365 = 76.4% of FY2025, or about 9.13 months.

Let me verify this calculation:
279 / 365 * 12 = 9.17 months

Hmm, let me recalculate:
- 279 days / 365 days = 0.7644
- 0.7644 * 12 months = 9.17 months

Or using average month length of 30.42 days (365/12):
- 279 / 30.42 = 9.17 months

So approximately 9.17 months, or about 9 months and 5 days.

Actually, let me be more careful. The question is about "months of FY2025." Let me think about this in terms of calendar months:

X Holdings merger closed March 28, 2025:
- Partial March (28-31): ~4 days
- Full months April through December: 9 months
- Total: 9 full months + partial March ≈ 9.13 months (using 4/31 of March)

Or if we count by days: 279 days out of 365 = 9.17 months (using 365/12 = 30.42 days/month)

Either way, approximately 9 months.

xAI merger closed February 2, 2026:
- This is after FY2025 ended
- 0 months of FY2025

Let me do the calculation precisely.

steps.message: I now have all the information needed to provide a complete answer.
steps.reasoning_content:
So:
- X Holdings: 279 days out of 365 = ~9.17 months (or 9 full months + 4 days of March ≈ 9.13 months)
- xAI: 0 months (merger closed February 2, 2026, after FY2025 ended)

Now let me compose the final answer with the analytical challenge of retrospective recast accounting.

arguments.final_result:
## Actual-Close Inclusion Periods in FY2025

SpaceX's fiscal year ends December 31, so FY2025 = January 1, 2025 through December 31, 2025 (365 days).

### xAI (acquired February 2, 2026)
- **0 months** of FY2025 on an actual-close basis.
- The xAI merger closed on February 2, 2026 — after FY2025 had already ended. xAI was therefore never legally/economically consolidated within SpaceX during any portion of FY2025.

### X Holdings (acquired March 28, 2025)
- **Approximately 9.13 months** (≈ 9 months and 4 days) of FY2025 on an actual-close basis.
- Calculation: March 28–31, 2025 (4 days of March) + April 1 through December 31, 2025 (9 full months) = 279 days out of 365 days in FY2025.
- 279 / 365 × 12 ≈ **9.17 months** (equivalently, 9 full months + 4/31 of March ≈ 9.13 months).

| Entity | Merger Effective Date | Actual-Close Months in FY2025 |
|---|---|---|
| xAI | February 2, 2026 | 0 months (closed after FYE 2025) |
| X Holdings | March 28, 2025 | ~9.13 months (279 of 365 days) |

## Analytical Challenge Created by Retrospective Recast Accounting

Because Elon Musk held a controlling financial interest in SpaceX, xAI, and X throughout the periods presented, the Mergers were accounted for as **reorganizations of entities under common control**. Under this accounting, the consolidated financial statements have been **retrospectively recast for all periods presented** to include the historical results of xAI and X at their historical carrying amounts (no new goodwill or intangibles recorded; intercompany transactions eliminated). The filing explicitly states this constitutes a **change in reporting entity** and that, as a result, **separate financial statements of xAI and X are not provided**.

This creates several material analytical challenges:

1. **Reported FY2025 figures do not reflect actual FY2025 economics.** The recast FY2025 income statement includes a full twelve months of xAI results, even though xAI was not actually consolidated until February 2, 2026 (zero actual-close months in FY2025). It also includes a full twelve months of X results, even though X was only consolidated from March 28, 2025 onward (~9.13 actual-close months). The "as-reported" FY2025 therefore overstates the actual combined economic activity that occurred under common ownership during that year.

2. **No standalone SpaceX baseline exists.** Because every historical period is recast to include xAI and X, there is no period in the filing that reflects SpaceX on a standalone basis. An analyst cannot isolate SpaceX's organic launch/services business performance from the contributions of the AI and social-media businesses.

3. **Year-over-year growth comparisons are distorted.** All comparative periods (FY2023, FY2024, FY2025) are presented on the same recast combined basis. Apparent growth or margin trends reflect the combined entity's economics rather than the organic trajectory of any single business, and the timing of when each entity's results were actually realized inside SpaceX is obscured.

4. **Inability to assess actual contribution during genuine consolidation periods.** Because xAI is shown for all of FY2025 despite not closing until 2026, and X is shown for all of FY2025 despite closing only in late March 2025, the face of the financials cannot be used to determine what SpaceX's actual consolidated results were during the periods these entities were genuinely part of the reporting group. Related-party transactions that occurred pre-merger (e.g., between SpaceX and X, or SpaceX and xAI) have been eliminated, further blurring the boundary between pre- and post-combination activity.

5. **Pro-forma-style presentation presented as "actual" history.** The recast presentation looks like a combined pro forma view but is presented as if it were actual historical results, which can mislead readers into treating the combined entity as having always existed in its current form. The economic substance — that these were separate Musk-controlled entities transacting at arm's length until the respective merger dates — is not visible in the reported numbers.

In short, retrospective common-control recasting means the FY2025 financials present a full-year combined xAI + X + SpaceX entity that, on an actual-close basis, never existed during FY2025: xAI contributed zero actual-close months and X contributed only ~9.13 months, yet both appear as if consolidated for the entire year. This makes it impossible to derive standalone SpaceX performance, true year-over-year organic growth, or the actual incremental contribution of each acquired business from the face of the recast financial statements alone.

    }
  ]
}

results.content:
## Actual-Close Inclusion Periods in FY2025

SpaceX's fiscal year ends December 31, so FY2025 = January 1, 2025 through December 31, 2025 (365 days).

### xAI (acquired February 2, 2026)
- **0 months** of FY2025 on an actual-close basis.
- The xAI merger closed on February 2, 2026 — after FY2025 had already ended. xAI was therefore never legally/economically consolidated within SpaceX during any portion of FY2025.

### X Holdings (acquired March 28, 2025)
- **Approximately 9.13 months** (≈ 9 months and 4 days) of FY2025 on an actual-close basis.
- Calculation: March 28–31, 2025 (4 days of March) + April 1 through December 31, 2025 (9 full months) = 279 days out of 365 days in FY2025.
- 279 / 365 × 12 ≈ **9.17 months** (equivalently, 9 full months + 4/31 of March ≈ 9.13 months).

| Entity | Merger Effective Date | Actual-Close Months in FY2025 |
|---|---|---|
| xAI | February 2, 2026 | 0 months (closed after FYE 2025) |
| X Holdings | March 28, 2025 | ~9.13 months (279 of 365 days) |

## Analytical Challenge Created by Retrospective Recast Accounting

Because Elon Musk held a controlling financial interest in SpaceX, xAI, and X throughout the periods presented, the Mergers were accounted for as **reorganizations of entities under common control**. Under this accounting, the consolidated financial statements have been **retrospectively recast for all periods presented** to include the historical results of xAI and X at their historical carrying amounts (no new goodwill or intangibles recorded; intercompany transactions eliminated). The filing explicitly states this constitutes a **change in reporting entity** and that, as a result, **separate financial statements of xAI and X are not provided**.

This creates several material analytical challenges:

1. **Reported FY2025 figures do not reflect actual FY2025 economics.** The recast FY2025 income statement includes a full twelve months of xAI results, even though xAI was not actually consolidated until February 2, 2026 (zero actual-close months in FY2025). It also includes a full twelve months of X results, even though X was only consolidated from March 28, 2025 onward (~9.13 actual-close months). The "as-reported" FY2025 therefore overstates the actual combined economic activity that occurred under common ownership during that year.

2. **No standalone SpaceX baseline exists.** Because every historical period is recast to include xAI and X, there is no period in the filing that reflects SpaceX on a standalone basis. An analyst cannot isolate SpaceX's organic launch/services business performance from the contributions of the AI and social-media businesses.

3. **Year-over-year growth comparisons are distorted.** All comparative periods (FY2023, FY2024, FY2025) are presented on the same recast combined basis. Apparent growth or margin trends reflect the combined entity's economics rather than the organic trajectory of any single business, and the timing of when each entity's results were actually realized inside SpaceX is obscured.

4. **Inability to assess actual contribution during genuine consolidation periods.** Because xAI is shown for all of FY2025 despite not closing until 2026, and X is shown for all of FY2025 despite closing only in late March 2025, the face of the financials cannot be used to determine what SpaceX's actual consolidated results were during the periods these entities were genuinely part of the reporting group. Related-party transactions that occurred pre-merger (e.g., between SpaceX and X, or SpaceX and xAI) have been eliminated, further blurring the boundary between pre- and post-combination activity.

5. **Pro-forma-style presentation presented as "actual" history.** The recast presentation looks like a combined pro forma view but is presented as if it were actual historical results, which can mislead readers into treating the combined entity as having always existed in its current form. The economic substance — that these were separate Musk-controlled entities transacting at arm's length until the respective merger dates — is not visible in the reported numbers.

In short, retrospective common-control recasting means the FY2025 financials present a full-year combined xAI + X + SpaceX entity that, on an actual-close basis, never existed during FY2025: xAI contributed zero actual-close months and X contributed only ~9.13 months, yet both appear as if consolidated for the entire year. This makes it impossible to derive standalone SpaceX performance, true year-over-year organic growth, or the actual incremental contribution of each acquired business from the face of the recast financial statements alone.
